Decompose per-row bytes + every index/secondary table + replication factor + WAL/compaction headroom (the real 3-6x multiplier), include the LARGEST table (often the events/analytics table), and give a rough MONTHLY $ figure naming the dominant cost driver (often egress/CDN or OLAP, not the primary DB). 4. STATE THE OBJECTIVE + SLOs AS NUMBERS up front (target percentile, scope/region, window) and what you are NOT optimizing; revisit them at the end. 5. DECOMPOSE THE p99 BUDGET across DNS/TLS, CDN, LB, app, cache, DB and each network hop so it sums to the SLO; separate the cache-HIT path from the cache-MISS (DB round-trip) and cross-region paths. 6. CONSISTENCY PER OPERATION AND PATH, not one global label. Explicitly trace READ-YOUR-OWN-WRITES: after a successful write, can the SAME actor see it on every path (cache miss, lagging replica, far region, cold edge)? If not, state the staleness bound and the mitigation (read-from-primary-after-write, version token). 7. IDEMPOTENCY ON EVERY MUTATING ENDPOINT — wire an idempotency/natural key + a dedup store with a TTL; define exactly what a retried request returns; and prove no shared namespace (auto-generated + user-chosen keys, per-region generators) can collide, defining the loser's behaviour. 8. NAME THE ONE BINDING BOTTLENECK under peak (including hot-key/celebrity single-shard QPS), quantify its ceiling, show the specific relief and the NEW ceiling; don't assume a uniform key distribution implies uniform traffic/storage. 9. EXAMINE EVERY SINGLE GLOBAL COORDINATOR (counter, allocator, sequencer, lock/leader, quorum): its throughput ceiling, cold-start/partition behaviour, what invariant breaks when it's down, and any holes/leakage from block-based grants. 10. MULTI-REGION / ASYNC: specify geo-routing AND write topology (single-write-region vs multi-master), cross-region write latency + replica-lag story, and the delivery guarantee (at-most / at-least / exactly-once) tied to a concrete accuracy SLA with a dedup/idempotent consumer. 11. FAILURE, DR, SECURITY, OPS AS FIRST-CLASS: per-dependency graceful degradation; RPO/RTO per data class + a tested restore; multi-AZ vs multi-region failover triggers; deploy/rollback + reversible migrations (expand/contract: add nullable → dual-write → batched backfill → switch reads → drop — never an in-place ALTER on a huge sharded table); authn AND authz (object-level: "can actor A read B's order?", not just "is A logged in") on every mutating endpoint + abuse defense + PII/retention/right-to-erasure (and how erasure propagates to replicas, caches, the search index, AND the append-only event log via crypto-shredding); SLO burn-rate alerts + tracing + runbooks. Express availability as a number of 9s. 12. BACK-PRESSURE & LOAD SHEDDING: for the hottest async path, state the bounded-queue behaviour (block / drop / spill-to-disk), the load-shedding ORDER under overload (shed reads and non-critical writes first, protect the money/correctness path), and the trigger signal (queue depth / consumer lag / p99 breach). Unbounded queues are a latent outage. 13. OBSERVABILITY: name the golden signals (latency, traffic, errors, saturation), the burn-rate alert threshold, and trace-id propagation end-to-end (gateway → services → queue → worker) so a single request is traceable across the async hop. 14. RATE-LIMITING / QUOTAS: for any public or abuse-prone endpoint, name the algorithm (token bucket / sliding window), WHERE the limiter state lives (and that it is itself a global coordinator — cross-ref invariant 9), and the client contract on rejection (HTTP 429 + Retry-After). 15. END WITH A TRADE-OFF LEDGER — the 2-3 decisions you are least sure of, what you gave up for each, and the scale change that would reverse them, tied back to the opening SLOs. Include the CAP/PACELC framing in one line (are we CP or AP for the core write path, and under normal operation do we favour latency or consistency?) so the textbook question is answerable cleanly. If an invariant genuinely can't be met for this problem, don't bury it — name it in a one-line "⚠️ Gaps I'd flag out loud" at the end of the opener and revisit it in the ledger. Saying your weak spot before the interviewer finds it is itself senior signal. Output format — use these exact ## headings, in order (opener streams FIRST as text). Everything below is the literal Markdown you must PRODUCE (the UI renders it). Most ## sections END with a "🎙️ Script:" block — the connected, read-aloud narration the candidate speaks; use 💬 lines for short say-while-you-draw moments, and gloss every term inline (jargon_guard). DE-DUPLICATION RULE (avoid bloat): state the headline number and the single core mechanism in FULL exactly ONCE — at §3 and its dedicated deep dive. Thereafter REFERENCE them in ≤6 words ("at our ~8K writes/s…"), never re-derive. Each per-section 🎙️ Script must add a NEW angle, not restate a prior section's number. If a §8 deep dive collapses to a single tier with no failure matrix or decision-forcing math, fold it into a related dive or the §7 consistency table rather than giving it its own header. ## 1. Scope, Requirements & Assumptions - Open with the 🎬 structure pitch (structure_pitch) as a 🎙️ line, then one line framing the core tension. - **🔒 Scope lock (do this FIRST, before requirements):** State the 2-4 questions whose answer FORKS the architecture — but phrase each as a *vetoable assumption*, not an open question: "I'll assume reads dominate ~100:1 and we're single-region to start — stop me if that's wrong." Ask ONLY the questions that CHANGE THE DESIGN (do we support group chat? is stock genuinely scarce? one region or global?); if a question doesn't fork the architecture, make it a one-line stated assumption instead — do NOT ask it. A long question list reads as junior; a senior states decisive assumptions and asks few sharp questions. End the scope lock with a checkpoint: "Here's what I'm treating as in scope and out of scope — add or cut before I commit?" The functional requirements below are DERIVED from these locked assumptions (show the link, e.g. "since we're assuming scarce stock, the core flows are…"). - **Functional (the core flows — "above the line"):** For EACH requirement, write THREE things: 1. The feature itself — "Users should be able to…" in one line. 2. **🗣️ Plain words:** one sentence a non-engineer could say — what does this mean in real life? (e.g. "this means someone can search 'red shoes' and get results in under a second, even if the stock count shown is a few seconds old"). This is what the candidate says when the interviewer asks 'why do we need this?' or 'can you explain that more simply?' 3. **⚡ Why it's hard:** one line on the engineering tension this flow introduces — what makes it non-trivial (e.g. "hard part: showing relevant results at scale without querying every product row"). Prioritise the flows that DEFINE the system, commonly 3-5. Do NOT artificially stop at 3 if a 4th/5th flow is genuinely core (e.g. ride-sharing: estimate, request, match, AND track/accept), and do NOT pad past ~5. Whatever you list here is EXACTLY what §6 builds — one diagrammed slice each — so list the real core set, no more and no fewer. Then a short **"Below the line (out of scope)"** list. - **Non-Functional (PROVISIONAL targets — tag each to the assumption it depends on):** For EACH quality, write THREE things. State the target as provisional and gated on a scope-lock assumption where relevant (e.g. "p99 ~200ms ASSUMING single-region reads — relaxes if we go multi-region"). 1. "The system should…" + an inline talkable target (availability as 9s, p99 latency, etc.) 2. **🗣️ Plain words:** what this target means in everyday English — e.g. "99.99% availability means the service is down for less than one hour per year total." 3. **💥 What breaks without it:** one line on the real-world consequence if we miss this target — e.g. "without this, a flash sale would oversell: two people buy the last unit, we lose money and trust." - If a rigor invariant can't be met for this problem, add the one-line "⚠️ Gaps I'd flag out loud". - 💬 line framing the tension; close with a 🎙️ Script narrating the requirements. ## 2. The Forks Behind My Assumptions (what changes if I'm wrong) This is the RESIDUAL of the scope lock from §1 — NOT a fresh round of questions. The scope-forking assumptions were already stated and locked in §1; here you make the 2-3 most consequential ones EXPLICIT as decision forks, so the interviewer can redirect and so you show you know where your design is sensitive. HARD NO-DUPLICATION RULE: do NOT re-ask any quantity, scale, or constraint you already asserted in §1. If §1 picked the branch (e.g. "assume 1M concurrent"), the question is settled — cut it. Asking "thousands or a million?" after §1 already assumed 1M is the exact bloat to avoid. Only surface a fork here if the answer would genuinely change the architecture AND you have NOT already committed to one branch. For EACH fork (2-3 max), write THREE things: 1. **The assumption I locked** — restated in one line ("I assumed scarce stock, so reservations are core"). 2. **↔️ What changes if I'm wrong:** two concrete bullets — "If actually unlimited → skip reservation logic entirely" / "If actually scarce → the reservation race is the hardest part" — showing the design delta. 3. **🗣️ How I'd say it:** one plain sentence the candidate says to invite a redirect ("if your traffic is really only thousands a second, tell me and I'll drop the sharding"). (Sections 1–2 are TEXT ONLY — no tools yet — so the candidate starts talking immediately.) End with a 🤝 Checkpoint. ## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers) First tool use allowed here. For EACH number you calculate, show the FULL DERIVATION as a visible arithmetic chain — the candidate must be able to explain HOW they got the number, not just WHAT it is. Format every row as: **Metric | Derivation (step-by-step math) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives** Example derivation format: "1B users × 0.1% DAU = 1M DAU; 1M × 10 reads/day ÷ 86,400s ≈ 116/s → round to ~120/s" Gloss every term inline the first time it appears (e.g. "QPS — queries per second, how many requests hit the server each second"; "TTL — time-to-live, how long a cached value is kept before it expires"). Name the ONE number that forces a design choice + its flip threshold (the point where the answer changes). Storage and non-decision numbers: one half-sentence aside only ("storage's a non-issue, a few TB"). End with a SHORT 🎙️ Script (2-4 sentences) that says OUT LOUD only the ONE or TWO decision-driving numbers — heavily rounded to a talkable phrase ("about 8 thousand reads a second", not "8,116/s") — plus the ratio/constraint they force and the conclusion ("so I'll spend my budget on reads, not writes"). The candidate should sound like an engineer making a point, not reading a spreadsheet. ## 4. Core Entities For EACH entity write THREE things, in a consistent format: - **Entity name** — one line: what it IS and what it OWNS (its job in the system). - 🗣️ **Simple analogy:** one sentence a non-engineer would understand — a real-world comparison (e.g. "think of the Reservation like a 'hold' tag on a product — someone put it in their cart and we've temporarily set it aside for them, but it goes back on the shelf if they don't pay in 10 minutes"). - ⚠️ **The interviewer probe:** one line on the most likely hard question about this entity — and a one-line answer (e.g. "they'll ask: 'how do you prevent two people from buying the last unit?' → answer: the Reservation entity creates an atomic hold before payment runs"). Tell the interviewer this is a first draft and you'll add full field definitions in the data model. Close with a 🎙️ Script that names the entities conversationally, flags the one most important/surprising one, and says what you'll add in the data model. ## 5. API / Interface Go one-by-one through the functional requirements and define the endpoint(s) that satisfy each — usually 1:1. For EACH endpoint write: - **Method + path** — and one sentence on WHY this HTTP verb (POST creates new state, GET only reads, DELETE removes, PUT replaces fully). This should be something the candidate can say out loud. - **Key request fields** (NOT a full schema — just the 2-4 fields that matter most): `fieldName: type` — one line WHY it exists (e.g. "`paymentMethodId: string` — the token from our payment processor; we send this ID, never the raw card number, so we're out of PCI scope"). If a field is intentionally ABSENT (e.g. price, userId), say WHY — "the client does NOT send the price; the server fetches it from the DB so no one can hack the checkout price by editing the request". - **Concrete example** — write a small JSON block showing a real example request body (for POST/PUT) and the corresponding response body. Use realistic values, not "string" placeholders. For GET endpoints, show the query parameters as a URL + the response JSON. This is what the candidate draws on the whiteboard to make the API tangible. Example format: ``` // Request POST /v1/orders { "paymentMethodId": "pm_abc123", "cartId": "cart_xyz789" } // Response 200 { "orderId": "ord_def456", "status": "PENDING", "estimatedAt": "2025-01-15T14:32:00Z" } ``` Include only the fields that matter; omit verbose boilerplate. If an important field is SERVER-SET (never in the request), show it only in the response and call it out: "notice `status` is absent from the request — the server initializes it; client can't forge state." - **One key design decision** on this endpoint — the thing you'd call out in an interview: idempotency key, why PENDING not synchronous, why a redirect vs a JSON response, etc. State it in plain words. Also state TWO cross-cutting API concerns once (not per endpoint): **VERSIONING** — prefix with `/v1`, and how you handle additive (new optional field, no version bump) vs breaking (new version) changes; and **PAGINATION** for any list endpoint — prefer a cursor/keyset token over offset/limit, with one line on why (offset gets slower and skips/dupes rows as data shifts under it). Default REST unless there's a reason not to; call out and justify non-obvious choices. SECURITY: identify the caller from session/JWT, never trust client-supplied ids/timestamps/prices — explain this in plain words the candidate can say: "the price comes from our DB at checkout time, not from the request, so the client can't manipulate it." Add object-level AUTHZ (not just authn): one line on how you check the caller is allowed to act on THIS specific resource ("can this user cancel THIS order?"). 💬 note + a 🎙️ Script walking the endpoints. ## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time) Build ONE subsection per above-the-line requirement from §1 — cover ALL of them, in priority order, start minimal, don't jump to scaling. CRITICAL: every §6.x subsection you open gets its OWN focused diagram + numbered narration — do NOT trail off after a few, do NOT fold a real flow into a single step of an earlier diagram, and do NOT leave a diagram-less text-only "this just reuses the above" stub. If a flow mostly reuses existing components, STILL draw the cumulative diagram and highlight the new edges/state it adds (e.g. the accept/decline transition, the navigation/track stream). A flow either earns a full diagrammed subsection here or it stays out of §6 — never a half-baked one. For EACH requirement, a subsection: ### 6.x "" - A one-sentence framing of this slice — what the user is trying to do and why this flow is interesting. - Introduce ONLY the new components this slice needs, wired onto what already exists. **ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE** — For EACH new component, write FIVE columns: **Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept** The "What it is" column must define the technology in one plain sentence as if explaining to a smart non-engineer — NO jargon without definition. The "Why THIS choice" column must name the specific reason this problem needs this tool (not generic "it scales" — something precise like "ES because we need ranked full-text search across 100M products in <100ms — no SQL can do that"). The "What we DIDN'T pick" column names 1-2 realistic alternatives and one concrete reason each was rejected (e.g. "Not SQL LIKE: would scan every product row on every search — 100M rows × every query = unusable"; "Not a custom inverted index: months of engineering with no relevance ranking"). Keep this to 2–5 rows per slice. Example format: | Search index | Elasticsearch — a search engine that pre-builds a map of words → products, so a query returns matches in ms | Need ranked full-text search across 100M products in under 100ms | Not SQL LIKE (scans every row, no ranking); not a hand-rolled index (months of work) | Lags behind catalog DB by a few seconds (fine for display) | THEN: a 🎙️ NARRATION block (not the table — this is what the candidate says OUT LOUD) that speaks each choice conversationally: define the technology briefly, say WHY you picked it, say what you considered instead and discarded, and state the trade-off you accepted. One short paragraph per component. The candidate reads this and should be able to say it naturally without memorizing jargon. **GLOSSES BLOCK** — After the table, write a dedicated "**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**" block. For EVERY technical term used in the table or this section (a service name, a pattern name, a protocol), write it as a standalone bolded entry with a plain-English definition + a one-line "why it matters here" sentence. Format: > **Elasticsearch** — [what it is in one simple sentence]. Here we use it because [one line]. > **CDC (Change Data Capture)** — [what it is]. Here we use it because [one line]. > **Kafka** — [what it is]. Here we use it because [one line]. Each entry must be self-contained — the candidate can read just that entry and understand the term. Never bundle multiple terms into one paragraph; each gets its own line. **DIAGRAM** — Include a diagram for this slice ONLY if it adds clarity that text alone cannot provide (a new component, a non-obvious data path, a branching flow). If this slice only adds one edge to existing components already fully drawn in a previous subsection, SKIP the diagram and write "↳ builds on §6.x diagram — no new components" instead. When you DO include one: - Use ` ```mermaid flowchart TD ` (top-down) by DEFAULT — this keeps the diagram narrow enough to fit a 13-inch MacBook Air screen without horizontal scrolling. Switch to `flowchart LR` ONLY for a dead-simple 3-4 node linear sequence (A→B→C, no branching) where vertical space would be wasted. - Use `subgraph` blocks to separate conceptually distinct paths (e.g. subgraph "Read Path" vs subgraph "Write / Sync Path"), with a readable title label on each subgraph. - Keep FOCUSED — only the components and edges this flow touches (~8-12 nodes total). - Color palette strictly: clients (blue #4A90D9 fill), our services (green #2E7D4F fill), async/queues (amber #B7791F fill), datastores as database[] cylinders (grey #4A5568 fill), external (purple #6B46C1 fill). ALL node fills must be dark enough to contrast with white text. - EVERY edge must have a label — not just a number, but a short description: `-->|"1: GET /search"|` not just `-->|1|`. Include WHAT is being sent or what the operation is. - Number the request arrows sequentially 1:1 with the prose narration below. - Add a one-line caption above the diagram (bold) naming the flow. - Directly below the diagram, a one-line `↳ reuses existing: (from §6.x)` note. **NUMBERED STEP NARRATION** — Walk through each numbered arrow in the diagram. For EACH step write: 1. What happens (the action) — in a complete sentence. 2. WHY this component handles it (not just "it goes there" but "because this service owns X"). 3. What STATE changes (what is different in the system after this step vs before). 4. What could go wrong here and how the system handles it (one line — the "what if" every interviewer asks about). This is the richest part of each slice. The candidate should be able to narrate every arrow on the diagram confidently. **💬 Say-while-drawing line** — one sentence that captures the KEY insight of this slice, phrased to say out loud while pointing at the diagram. Should name the core tension and the design decision that resolves it. **🎙️ Script** — 3–5 connected sentences narrating this slice end-to-end in plain English. Should sound like someone explaining to a colleague, not reading a textbook. Must: define any jargon the first time it appears, state the one architectural decision that makes this slice work, name the trade-off and why it's acceptable. End with the ONE full diagram captioned "Final (high-level)" — it MUST be the exact RECONCILED UNION of all the slices: EVERY component that appeared in ANY slice (6.1, 6.2, …) is present here with its same name and color (NOTHING dropped — never lose a cache, DB, or queue that a slice introduced), and it introduces NOTHING that wasn't built in a slice. Use subgraphs in the final diagram to group the "Read Path" components vs "Write / Transaction Path" vs "Async / Events" path so the visual tells the story at a glance. Before finalizing, mentally diff it against the slices and confirm nothing is missing. Annotate load-bearing schema columns inline next to the relevant datastore, then a 🎙️ Script narrating the full flow (2–3 sentences per path, not one blob) and a 🤝 Checkpoint handing the deep-dive choice to the interviewer. ## 7. Data Model & Storage **PART A — ENTITY TABLE:** For each entity, one row with: Entity | Key fields (name + type, annotate the PK/shard key) | Chosen store | Shard/partition key | Consistency guarantee Keep field lists tight — only the columns that matter for access patterns or the deep dives. **PART B — STORAGE DECISION CARDS:** After the table, for EACH distinct storage choice (each different database/store used), write a standalone "decision card" block. Each card has FIVE parts: **🗄️ [Store name] — used for: [entities]** 1. **What it is (plain words):** one sentence defining this store as if explaining to a non-engineer. (e.g. "PostgreSQL is a relational database — it stores data in rows and columns with strict rules, and it guarantees that a multi-step operation either fully succeeds or fully rolls back.") 2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern that forces this choice:** Be specific — name the EXACT reason this problem needs THIS store. Not "it scales" but "we need an atomic 'decrement-and-check' operation that prevents overselling, and only a relational DB with row-level locking can guarantee that two buyers racing for the last unit can't both win." 3. **What we considered instead and why we rejected each:** List 2-3 realistic alternatives with a one-line rejection reason each. Make the rejections concrete — not "it doesn't scale" but "DynamoDB would work for the cart but NOT for inventory — it can't do the conditional atomic decrement we need to prevent overselling without a lot of custom application-side logic." E.g.: - **MongoDB** — flexible schema is nice, but we need ACID multi-row transactions for the order+inventory+payment saga; Mongo's transactions are slower and less battle-tested here. - **DynamoDB** — great for carts (KV by user) but no native conditional decrement strong enough for inventory; also vendor lock-in. - **MySQL** — identical to Postgres for this use case; Postgres has better JSON support and is the standard choice in modern stacks. 4. **Trade-off we accept:** one line on what we're giving up (e.g. "operational complexity of managing shards; query joins across shards are impossible so we denormalize"). 5. **🗣️ How to say it out loud:** 2-3 natural sentences the candidate can say verbatim — casual, not textbook. (e.g. "For orders and inventory I'm using Postgres, because this is money and I can't afford a half-committed transaction. I looked at Dynamo — it's great for the cart — but it can't do the atomic stock decrement I need without extra complexity. The trade-off is I have to shard manually, but that's fine because I shard by order_id and each shard is independent.") **PART C — Per-operation consistency summary:** one compact table: Operation | Store | Consistency level | Why that level is right here Cover: the writes that MUST be strong (money, inventory), the reads that can be eventual (display, search), and the read-your-own-writes edge case. **PART D — DATA LIFECYCLE:** For the LARGEST / fastest-growing table (the one from rigor invariant 3 — usually events/analytics/logs), state the retention window and tie it back to the §3 monthly storage $ figure ("at ~50TB/month, 90-day retention caps us at ~150TB — without it we grow unbounded"). Name the reclamation mechanism: time-partitioned tables with a PARTITION DROP (O(1), instant) rather than row-by-row DELETE (which churns the index and never reclaims space cleanly), plus where cold data goes (S3/Glacier archival tier) if it must be kept. One line is enough; the point is showing the big table has an answer to "then what?". **🎙️ Script:** 4-6 sentences walking the whole storage tier conversationally — mention the dominant choice (usually Postgres for the core), the KV store, the cache, and the event log. Must name one "I considered X but rejected it because Y" to signal senior-level thinking. ## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great (the senior signal) This is where the interview is won. Start with a one-line 🆘 "If you get stuck" recovery pointer. Pick the 3–5 hardest problems (driven by the non-functional requirements and edge cases). Pose EACH as a question header: **"### How do we [problem]?"** For EACH deep dive, escalate through tiers. Each tier has a FIXED structure — follow it exactly: **#### [Bad / Good / Great]: ** *(For Good and Great tiers only)* **↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** Open with one sentence saying exactly what broke in the tier before and WHY this new approach fixes that specific thing. This makes the progression feel like logical problem-solving, not a random list of techniques. E.g. for Good after Bad: "Bad held the lock across the payment call — so I split it: reserve first in a millisecond, pay second, outside any lock." Never skip this line in Good or Great. **🗣️ What this is in plain words:** One sentence explaining the approach as if to a smart non-engineer who has never heard of it. No jargon without definition. This line comes FIRST, before any technical description, so the candidate understands what they're about to describe. **Approach:** Explain what you actually do in this tier — the mechanism. Be specific about the steps. Every technical term used here must be defined inline the FIRST time it appears in this deep dive (not just in the glosses — right here, parenthetically: e.g. "a row lock (a database mechanism that prevents any other transaction from reading or writing that row until you release it)"). **Why this seemed reasonable / Why people try this:** One line — the intuition behind this approach. The candidate should be able to say "I tried X first because it felt natural — it's what most people reach for." **⚠️ What breaks (Challenges):** Be concrete — not "it doesn't scale" but "at 500K buyers racing for one item during a flash sale, the lock is held across a payment call that takes 300ms, so 500K threads are now waiting — the database connection pool (the fixed set of open connections the app keeps — typically 50-200) exhausts in seconds, and the whole site stops responding." Name the numbers. Name the failure mode. Name why this matters for THIS system. **🔁 What forces the upgrade to the next tier:** one sentence — what exact scenario makes this tier break that the next tier fixes. (Omit on the final Great tier — replace with the failure matrix or trade-offs instead.) Tiers are OPTIONAL — skip Bad if the answer is obvious; don't manufacture tiers. For EACH MEANINGFUL tier (usually Good and Great), include a titled mermaid diagram showing ONLY the mechanism for that tier — not the whole system. For the Great tier also include: **🔢 Decision-forcing math (if applicable):** Show a quick calculation that proves why this tier matters — e.g. "500K buyers × 300ms lock hold ÷ 200 connection pool = exhaustion in under 1 second." Keep it rounded and talkable. **✅ Failure matrix (for correctness-critical deep dives):** A compact table of every failure scenario and what happens — e.g. "Payment succeeds but commit crashes → reconcile by idempotency key → complete commit, no double-charge." Cover: success, failure, timeout, retry, partial failure, crash-after-payment. Each deep dive ends with: - **🎙️ Script:** 3-5 connected sentences saying the Great solution out loud, in plain conversational English. Define every term the first time. Sound like an engineer explaining to a colleague, not reading slides. - **🧠 "If they ask…":** the single most obvious pushback and a 2-sentence answer. Cover where relevant: the binding bottleneck + its relief + the new ceiling after the fix; hot-key / celebrity-item problems; idempotency and duplicate suppression; every global coordinator (anything that is a single point of serialization); consistency stance (strong vs eventual) and read-your-own-writes; what happens when each piece fails. ## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost Structure this as FOUR named sub-sections, each with plain-English definitions for every term: **9A — Availability targets (what the "nines" mean):** For each path (read path, money/write path), state the availability target as "X nines" AND what that means in human terms: "four nines = 52 minutes of downtime per year total; three nines = 8.7 hours." Then state the mechanisms that achieve it (CDN, replicas, multi-AZ failover). Make the mechanism readable: define "multi-AZ" (multiple data centres in the same region so if one building loses power the other takes over automatically) the first time it appears. **9B — Per-dependency failure table:** For EACH major component, one row: Component | What breaks if it goes down | How the system degrades gracefully | Recovery action. The "graceful degradation" column must describe what the USER SEES — not just "falls back to replica" but "users can still browse categories but search results might be slightly stale." Define every fallback mechanism inline (e.g. "serve from Redis cache (the in-memory store) until the search cluster recovers"). **9C — RPO / RTO per data class (plain definitions FIRST):** Define RPO and RTO in plain words before using them: "RPO (Recovery Point Objective) = how much data we're OK losing if a crash happens right now. RPO≈0 means we lose nothing — every write is synchronously replicated before we acknowledge it. RTO (Recovery Time Objective) = how long before the service is working again after a crash." Then give a table: Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism. Cover: orders/payments (RPO≈0), catalog (looser — can rebuild from source), search index (rebuildable, minutes of lag OK). **9D — Cost breakdown:** Name the top 3 cost drivers and WHY each is expensive in plain terms (e.g. "CDN egress — every byte of image data served to a user's browser is charged by the CDN; at millions of page views a day this is the biggest line item, not the database"). Give a rough monthly order-of-magnitude. Name the one optimization that would cut cost the most. **🎙️ Script:** 3-4 sentences on the failure story — what the user EXPERIENCES when each major thing fails, not just what the system does internally. ## 10. Trade-off Ledger For EACH of the 2–4 most significant design decisions, write a DECISION CARD with five parts: **🔀 Decision: [what you chose] vs [what you didn't]** 1. **What we chose and why:** one line — the core reason. 2. **What we gave up:** one concrete thing that's harder or worse because of this choice. 3. **🗣️ Plain words:** one sentence a non-engineer can understand — "think of it like choosing a fast highway that's slightly less reliable over a slower but guaranteed road." 4. **When this reverses:** the exact condition that would make you flip this decision — be specific ("if flash sales are removed from scope, we drop the Redis counter and go back to a single Postgres row — simpler, no reconciliation needed"; "if we owned the payment processor in-house, we could do a real two-phase commit instead of a saga"). 5. **🗣️ How to say it:** one sentence the candidate can say out loud — natural, not textbook. Tie each decision back to the non-functional requirements and any "⚠️ Gaps" from §1. **🎙️ Script:** 3-4 sentences walking the ledger — the decisions, the trade-offs, and what would make you change your mind. ## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers Cover ALL of these question domains — generate 2-3 questions per domain, 12-18 total: (1) Core algorithm / the hardest part of THIS specific problem (e.g. "two buyers race for the last unit — who wins?"), (2) Failure scenarios ("what if the payment processor goes down?", "what if Redis crashes?"), (3) Scale / hot-spot ("how do you handle a flash sale?"), (4) Consistency / correctness ("can the same order be placed twice?", "can you oversell?"), (5) Security ("how do you stop price tampering?", "what if someone forges a user id?"), (6) Cost ("what's the most expensive part?", "how would you cut costs?"), (7) Extensibility ("how would you add feature X?", "how would you support Y?"). Do NOT generate only checkout/core questions — a real interview covers all domains. For EACH question, write FOUR parts: **❓ [The question exactly as an interviewer would say it]** **The mechanism (what actually happens):** 2-3 sentences. Every term used that hasn't been defined earlier must be defined inline here. **🗣️ In plain words:** 1-2 sentences — same answer, zero jargon. This is what the candidate says if they blank on the technical version: "In simple terms, [plain version]." **💬 One-liner to say out loud:** the sharpest single sentence — confident, not textbook. Answers must be REAL and SPECIFIC — not "we use caching" but "the product page is in Redis with a 60-second TTL; during a flash sale the cache absorbs ~8K requests/s so Postgres never sees the spike." No one-liners as the main answer. Mechanism + plain words + one-liner every time. End with a **🎙️ "60-second verbal summary"** — follow this template exactly, filling in the specifics for this problem: "[1 sentence: what the system is in plain words]. The design splits into two halves. [2-3 sentences: the READ HALF — what it is, what technologies, why it works]. [2-3 sentences: the WRITE / TRANSACTION HALF — what it is, the hardest problem in it, and the one mechanism that solves it]. [1 sentence: how the two halves connect — usually an event stream or async pipeline]. [1 sentence: the single design principle that every hard decision traces back to]." Write this as flowing connected sentences — no bullet points, no section headers. It should sound like someone giving a confident wrap-up to a colleague, not reading notes.

content.text: I've read both files in full. Now I understand the exact structure required. Let me set up the workspace and compute the capacity numbers first.
Now let me write the capacity.py script with the payment-gateway-specific numbers.

input.content:
"""
Capacity model for a Payment Gateway System (HLD).

We process payments (authorize / capture / refund) through external processors,
with idempotency, a double-entry ledger, async webhooks, and PCI tokenization.

The binding constraint is NOT raw QPS — payments are low-volume vs a social feed.
The binding constraints are:
  (1) correctness under retries (idempotency) — drives a dedup store,
  (2) ledger write durability + the largest table (ledger_entries) storage,
  (3) external-processor latency dominating the synchronous p99 budget.

Everything is rounded to talkable phrases at print time.
"""

SECONDS_PER_DAY = 86_400

def rnd(x):
    """Round to a talkable phrase."""
    if x >= 1_000_000_000:
        return f"~{x/1_000_000_000:.1f}B"
    if x >= 1_000_000:
        return f"~{x/1_000_000:.1f}M"
    if x >= 1_000:
        return f"~{x/1_000:.1f}K"
    return f"~{x:.0f}"

print("=" * 70)
print("PAYMENT GATEWAY — CAPACITY MODEL")
print("=" * 70)

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# 1. Traffic: payment volume
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Assume a mid-large gateway: merchants pushing ~50M successful payments/day.
# (Stripe does on the order of hundreds of millions/day at peak; 50M/day is a
#  defensible "serious but not hyperscale" target that still forks the design.)
payments_per_day = 50_000_000
avg_payment_tps = payments_per_day / SECONDS_PER_DAY
print(f"\n[Traffic] Payments/day               = {payments_per_day:,}")
print(f"[Traffic] Average payment TPS         = {avg_payment_tps:,.0f}  -> {rnd(avg_payment_tps)}/s")

# Peak burst factor: payments are spiky (Black Friday, midnight subscription
# renewals, regional lunch-hour). Use 5x average as the design peak.
BURST = 5
peak_payment_tps = avg_payment_tps * BURST
print(f"[Traffic] Burst factor                = {BURST}x")
print(f"[Traffic] PEAK payment TPS            = {peak_payment_tps:,.0f}  -> {rnd(peak_payment_tps)}/s   <-- design target")

# Each payment is not one request. A full charge lifecycle =
#   authorize + capture (sometimes 1 combined) + ~N webhook deliveries +
#   status reads by the merchant. Model the request fan-out:
WRITES_PER_PAYMENT = 3      # authorize, capture, ledger-post (internal mutations)
READS_PER_PAYMENT  = 10     # merchant polls status, dashboard, reconciliation reads
WEBHOOKS_PER_PAYMENT = 2    # payment.succeeded, charge.captured (at-least-once -> may retry)

write_tps_peak = peak_payment_tps * WRITES_PER_PAYMENT
read_tps_peak  = peak_payment_tps * READS_PER_PAYMENT
webhook_tps_peak = peak_payment_tps * WEBHOOKS_PER_PAYMENT
read_write_ratio = READS_PER_PAYMENT / WRITES_PER_PAYMENT

print(f"\n[Mix] Peak WRITE tps (mutations)      = {write_tps_peak:,.0f} -> {rnd(write_tps_peak)}/s")
print(f"[Mix] Peak READ tps (status/dashboard)= {read_tps_peak:,.0f} -> {rnd(read_tps_peak)}/s")
print(f"[Mix] Peak WEBHOOK tps               = {webhook_tps_peak:,.0f} -> {rnd(webhook_tps_peak)}/s")
print(f"[Mix] Read:Write ratio               = {read_write_ratio:.1f}:1  (writes dominate the RISK, not the volume)")

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# 2. THE decision-forcing number: write TPS vs a single Postgres primary
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# A single well-tuned Postgres primary on durable money writes (fsync, synchronous
# replica) sustains roughly ~5K-10K write tps before commit latency degrades.
# We are well above that on writes, so the ledger MUST shard.
PG_PRIMARY_WRITE_CEILING = 8_000   # talkable: "a Postgres primary tops out ~8K money-writes/s"
print("\n" + "-" * 70)
print("DECISION-FORCING NUMBER: ledger write throughput vs one Postgres primary")
print("-" * 70)
print(f"Peak ledger writes/s                  = {write_tps_peak:,.0f} -> {rnd(write_tps_peak)}/s")
print(f"Single Postgres primary ceiling       = {PG_PRIMARY_WRITE_CEILING:,} writes/s")
shards_needed = -(-int(write_tps_peak) // PG_PRIMARY_WRITE_CEILING)  # ceil
print(f"=> Shards needed (ceil)               = {shards_needed}  shards by merchant_id")
print(f"FLIP THRESHOLD: below ~{PG_PRIMARY_WRITE_CEILING:,} writes/s a SINGLE primary works — don't shard.")
print(f"               above it, shard the ledger by merchant_id. We are at {rnd(write_tps_peak)}/s.")

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# 3. Idempotency dedup store sizing
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Every mutating request carries an idempotency key; we store key -> result for a
# TTL window (24h) so retries return the original result instead of re-charging.
IDEMP_TTL_HOURS = 24
keys_per_day = payments_per_day * WRITES_PER_PAYMENT   # each mutation keyed
IDEMP_ROW_BYTES = 512   # key + stored response snapshot + status + ts
idemp_rows = keys_per_day * (IDEMP_TTL_HOURS / 24)
idemp_bytes = idemp_rows * IDEMP_ROW_BYTES
print("\n" + "-" * 70)
print("IDEMPOTENCY DEDUP STORE (Redis, 24h TTL)")
print("-" * 70)
print(f"Keyed mutations/day                   = {keys_per_day:,} -> {rnd(keys_per_day)}")
print(f"Rows live at any time (24h TTL)       = {idemp_rows:,.0f} -> {rnd(idemp_rows)}")
print(f"Bytes per row                         = {IDEMP_ROW_BYTES}")
print(f"Dedup store footprint                 = {idemp_bytes/1e9:.1f} GB  -> fits in RAM on a small Redis cluster")

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# 4. Storage: the largest table is ledger_entries (double-entry => 2+ rows/txn)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Double-entry: each payment posts >= 2 ledger rows (debit + credit); refunds,
# fees, payouts add more. Model ~4 ledger rows per payment.
LEDGER_ROWS_PER_PAYMENT = 4
LEDGER_ROW_BYTES = 256          # account_id, txn_id, amount, currency, dr/cr, ts, hash-chain ptr
ledger_rows_per_day = payments_per_day * LEDGER_ROWS_PER_PAYMENT
ledger_bytes_per_day = ledger_rows_per_day * LEDGER_ROW_BYTES

# Real footprint multiplier: indexes (by account, by txn, by time) + replication (3x)
#   + WAL/compaction headroom. Use ~4x.
FOOTPRINT_MULT = 4
ledger_raw_per_year = ledger_bytes_per_day * 365
ledger_full_per_year = ledger_raw_per_year * FOOTPRINT_MULT

print("\n" + "-" * 70)
print("LARGEST TABLE: ledger_entries (double-entry, append-only, immutable)")
print("-" * 70)
print(f"Ledger rows/payment                   = {LEDGER_ROWS_PER_PAYMENT}")
print(f"Ledger rows/day                       = {ledger_rows_per_day:,} -> {rnd(ledger_rows_per_day)}")
print(f"Raw bytes/day                         = {ledger_bytes_per_day/1e9:.1f} GB/day")
print(f"Raw bytes/year                        = {ledger_raw_per_year/1e12:.1f} TB/year (raw)")
print(f"Full footprint (x{FOOTPRINT_MULT} idx+repl+WAL)   = {ledger_full_per_year/1e12:.1f} TB/year")
print("Driver: ledger is APPEND-ONLY and never deleted (audit/regulatory) -> grows unbounded;")
print("        hot 90 days in Postgres, older partitions to cold object storage.")

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# 5. p99 latency budget for the synchronous authorize path
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# The external processor call dominates. Decompose a ~1200ms p99 budget.
print("\n" + "-" * 70)
print("p99 LATENCY BUDGET — synchronous authorize path (target ~1200ms)")
print("-" * 70)
budget = {
    "TLS + gateway + authz (JWT)":      30,
    "idempotency check (Redis)":         5,
    "ledger pre-auth write (Postgres)": 20,
    "EXTERNAL PROCESSOR call (Stripe)": 1000,   # <-- dominates
    "capture-result ledger post":       20,
    "response serialize + network":     25,
}
total = sum(budget.values())
for k, v in budget.items():
    print(f"  {k:<38} {v:>5} ms")
print(f"  {'-'*38} {'-'*5}")
print(f"  {'TOTAL p99':<38} {total:>5} ms  -> ~{round(total,-2):.0f} ms")
print(f"  => The processor call is {budget['EXTERNAL PROCESSOR call (Stripe)']/total*100:.0f}% of the budget.")
print("  => CONCLUSION: capture must be ASYNC; never make the merchant wait on a slow processor.")

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# 6. Cost: top drivers + unit economics ($ per 1M transactions)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
print("\n" + "-" * 70)
print("COST — order of magnitude/month + unit economics")
print("-" * 70)
# Rough monthly infra (NOT processor interchange fees, which are pass-through):
ledger_db_monthly   = 60_000   # sharded Postgres primaries+replicas, multi-AZ, provisioned IOPS
redis_monthly       = 8_000    # idempotency + cache cluster
kafka_monthly       = 15_000   # webhook + event bus, durable, replicated
compute_monthly     = 40_000   # API + worker + webhook-delivery fleets
cold_storage_monthly= 5_000    # ledger archive in object storage
observability_monthly = 12_000 # tracing + metrics + audit log retention
monthly_total = (ledger_db_monthly + redis_monthly + kafka_monthly +
                 compute_monthly + cold_storage_monthly + observability_monthly)
print(f"  Ledger DB (sharded Postgres, multi-AZ)   ${ledger_db_monthly:>7,}/mo  <-- top driver")
print(f"  Compute (API + workers + webhook fleet)  ${compute_monthly:>7,}/mo")
print(f"  Kafka event/webhook bus                  ${kafka_monthly:>7,}/mo")
print(f"  Observability + audit retention          ${observability_monthly:>7,}/mo")
print(f"  Redis (idempotency + cache)              ${redis_monthly:>7,}/mo")
print(f"  Cold ledger archive (object storage)     ${cold_storage_monthly:>7,}/mo")
print(f"  {'-'*42}")
print(f"  MONTHLY INFRA TOTAL                      ${monthly_total:>7,}/mo  -> order ~$140K/mo")

monthly_txns = payments_per_day * 30
cost_per_million = monthly_total / (monthly_txns / 1_000_000)
print(f"\n  Monthly transactions                     = {monthly_txns:,} -> {rnd(monthly_txns)}")
print(f"  UNIT ECONOMICS: infra cost per 1M txns   = ${cost_per_million:,.2f}  per 1M transactions")
print(f"  (i.e. ~{cost_per_million/1_000_000*100:.4f} cents of infra per transaction — interchange fees dwarf this)")

print("\n" + "=" * 70)
print("SUMMARY OF TALKABLE NUMBERS")
print("=" * 70)
print(f"  Peak payments         : {rnd(peak_payment_tps)}/s")
print(f"  Peak ledger writes    : {rnd(write_tps_peak)}/s  -> shard into {shards_needed} (>{PG_PRIMARY_WRITE_CEILING:,}/s ceiling)")
print(f"  Read:write            : ~{read_write_ratio:.0f}:1")
print(f"  Ledger growth         : {ledger_full_per_year/1e12:.0f} TB/year (full footprint)")
print(f"  Sync p99              : ~{round(total,-2):.0f} ms (processor = {budget['EXTERNAL PROCESSOR call (Stripe)']/total*100:.0f}%)")
print(f"  Infra cost            : ~${round(monthly_total,-3):,}/mo, ~${cost_per_million:,.0f}/1M txns")

1	"""Capacity model for a Payment Gateway System.
2	
3	We model a mid-to-large payment gateway (Stripe-class but smaller): merchants
4	send us charges, we tokenize cards, call external processors (Visa/MC rails via
5	acquirers), persist a double-entry ledger, and fire async webhooks to merchants.
6	
7	Everything here is the source of the rounded numbers used in the markdown.
8	"""
9	
10	SEC_PER_DAY = 86_400
11	SEC_PER_MONTH = SEC_PER_DAY * 30
12	
13	
14	def rnd(n):
15	    """Round to a talkable magnitude."""
16	    if n >= 1_000_000_000:
17	        return f"~{n/1_000_000_000:.1f}B"
18	    if n >= 1_000_000:
19	        return f"~{n/1_000_000:.1f}M"
20	    if n >= 1_000:
21	        return f"~{n/1_000:.1f}K"
22	    return f"~{n:.1f}"
23	
24	
25	print("=" * 64)
26	print("PAYMENT GATEWAY — CAPACITY MODEL")
27	print("=" * 64)
28	
29	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
30	# 1. Transaction volume (the workload driver)
31	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
32	# Assume: 2B payment transactions / month at steady state (large gateway,
33	# Stripe-class). A "transaction" = one authorize (+ usually a capture). Refunds
34	# + disputes are a small tail. This is the headline workload.
35	txns_per_month = 2_000_000_000
36	avg_payment_qps = txns_per_month / SEC_PER_MONTH
37	print("\n[1] PAYMENT WRITE QPS (the money path)")
38	print(f"  {txns_per_month:,} txns/month / {SEC_PER_MONTH:,}s")
39	print(f"  = {avg_payment_qps:,.0f} authorize/s average")
40	
41	# Peak: payments are spiky (Black Friday, midnight subscription renewals,
42	# flash sales). Use a burst factor of 10x over the monthly average — payments
43	# are far burstier than reads.
44	BURST = 10
45	peak_payment_qps = avg_payment_qps * BURST
46	print(f"  burst factor {BURST}x -> {peak_payment_qps:,.0f} authorize/s PEAK")
47	print(f"  ROUNDED: avg {rnd(avg_payment_qps)}/s, PEAK {rnd(peak_payment_qps)}/s")
48	
49	# Each authorize is typically followed by exactly one capture (immediate or
50	# delayed). Plus refunds ~3% of payments, disputes ~0.3%.
51	captures_qps = avg_payment_qps  # 1:1 with auth in the common case
52	refund_qps = avg_payment_qps * 0.03
53	dispute_qps = avg_payment_qps * 0.003
54	# Total ledger-mutating ops at peak:
55	ledger_writes_avg = avg_payment_qps + captures_qps + refund_qps + dispute_qps
56	ledger_writes_peak = ledger_writes_avg * BURST
57	print("\n[2] LEDGER WRITE QPS (double-entry — every money event)")
58	print(f"  auth {avg_payment_qps:,.0f} + capture {captures_qps:,.0f}"
59	      f" + refund {refund_qps:,.0f} + dispute {dispute_qps:,.1f}")
60	print(f"  = {ledger_writes_avg:,.0f} money-events/s avg")
61	print(f"  PEAK {ledger_writes_peak:,.0f}/s")
62	# Double-entry: each money event writes >= 2 ledger rows (debit + credit).
63	ledger_rows_peak = ledger_writes_peak * 2
64	print(f"  x2 rows (debit+credit) = {ledger_rows_peak:,.0f} ledger-row writes/s PEAK")
65	print(f"  ROUNDED: {rnd(ledger_writes_peak)} money-events/s, "
66	      f"{rnd(ledger_rows_peak)} ledger rows/s at peak")
67	
68	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
69	# 3. Idempotency dedup store sizing (sized from WRITE qps, TTL'd)
70	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
71	# Every mutating request carries an idempotency key. We keep keys 24h.
72	IDEMP_TTL_H = 24
73	# All mutating requests (auth + capture + refund + dispute) at AVG (store is
74	# sized for sustained load, not instantaneous burst, but we keep headroom).
75	idemp_writes_avg = ledger_writes_avg
76	idemp_keys_stored = idemp_writes_avg * 3600 * IDEMP_TTL_H
77	# Each idempotency record: key (36B) + status + response snapshot (~1KB).
78	IDEMP_REC_BYTES = 1_200
79	idemp_bytes = idemp_keys_stored * IDEMP_REC_BYTES
80	print("\n[3] IDEMPOTENCY DEDUP STORE (Redis, 24h TTL)")
81	print(f"  {idemp_writes_avg:,.0f} keys/s x {3600*IDEMP_TTL_H:,}s")
82	print(f"  = {idemp_keys_stored:,.0f} live keys")
83	print(f"  x {IDEMP_REC_BYTES}B/rec = {idemp_bytes/1e9:.1f} GB")
84	print(f"  ROUNDED: {rnd(idemp_keys_stored)} live keys, ~{idemp_bytes/1e9:.0f} GB in Redis")
85	
86	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
87	# 4. Ledger storage (the LARGEST, fastest-growing table) — full footprint
88	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
89	# Each ledger entry row: ~300 bytes raw (ids, amount, currency, account refs,
90	# timestamps, txn ref). Two rows per money event.
91	LEDGER_ROW_BYTES = 300
92	ledger_rows_per_month = ledger_writes_avg * 2 * SEC_PER_MONTH
93	raw_ledger_bytes_month = ledger_rows_per_month * LEDGER_ROW_BYTES
94	# Real footprint multiplier: indexes (PK + account_id + txn_id + time) ~2x,
95	# replication factor 3, WAL + compaction headroom ~1.5x.
96	INDEX_MULT = 2.0
97	REPLICA = 3
98	WAL_MULT = 1.5
99	footprint_mult = INDEX_MULT * REPLICA * WAL_MULT
100	ledger_footprint_month = raw_ledger_bytes_month * footprint_mult
101	print("\n[4] LEDGER STORAGE (largest table — full footprint)")
102	print(f"  {ledger_rows_per_month:,.0f} rows/month x {LEDGER_ROW_BYTES}B")
103	print(f"  = {raw_ledger_bytes_month/1e12:.2f} TB raw/month")
104	print(f"  x footprint mult {footprint_mult:.1f} "
105	      f"(idx {INDEX_MULT} x replica {REPLICA} x wal {WAL_MULT})")
106	print(f"  = {ledger_footprint_month/1e12:.2f} TB/month full footprint")
107	# Retention: regulatory (card scheme + tax) typically 7 years for the ledger,
108	# but HOT (queryable in Postgres) we keep ~13 months; older partitions go to S3.
109	HOT_MONTHS = 13
110	ledger_hot_tb = ledger_footprint_month / 1e12 * HOT_MONTHS
111	print(f"  HOT {HOT_MONTHS} months in Postgres = {ledger_hot_tb:.1f} TB")
112	# Monthly $ for hot ledger storage on cloud block storage (~$0.10/GB-mo SSD).
113	SSD_GB_MO = 0.10
114	ledger_storage_cost = ledger_hot_tb * 1000 * SSD_GB_MO
115	print(f"  hot storage $ = {ledger_hot_tb:.1f}TB x 1000 x ${SSD_GB_MO}/GB-mo "
116	      f"= ${ledger_storage_cost:,.0f}/mo")
117	print(f"  ROUNDED: ~{ledger_footprint_month/1e12:.0f} TB/month footprint, "
118	      f"~{ledger_hot_tb:.0f} TB hot, ~${ledger_storage_cost/1000:.0f}K/mo SSD")
119	
120	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
121	# 5. Webhook fan-out (async, at-least-once)
122	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
123	# Each payment lifecycle emits multiple webhook events: auth, capture, and
124	# possibly refund/dispute. Assume ~3 webhook deliveries per payment on average.
125	WEBHOOKS_PER_PAYMENT = 3
126	webhook_qps_avg = avg_payment_qps * WEBHOOKS_PER_PAYMENT
127	webhook_qps_peak = webhook_qps_avg * BURST
128	print("\n[5] WEBHOOK DELIVERY QPS (async, at-least-once)")
129	print(f"  {avg_payment_qps:,.0f} payments/s x {WEBHOOKS_PER_PAYMENT} events")
130	print(f"  = {webhook_qps_avg:,.0f} webhooks/s avg, PEAK {webhook_qps_peak:,.0f}/s")
131	# Retry amplification: ~5% of endpoints are slow/down and get retried up to
132	# 8 times with backoff -> amplification factor.
133	RETRY_FRACTION = 0.05
134	RETRY_ATTEMPTS = 8
135	retry_amp = 1 + RETRY_FRACTION * (RETRY_ATTEMPTS - 1)
136	webhook_attempts_peak = webhook_qps_peak * retry_amp
137	print(f"  retry amp {retry_amp:.2f}x ({RETRY_FRACTION:.0%} endpoints x "
138	      f"{RETRY_ATTEMPTS} attempts)")
139	print(f"  = {webhook_attempts_peak:,.0f} delivery attempts/s PEAK")
140	print(f"  ROUNDED: {rnd(webhook_qps_peak)} webhooks/s peak, "
141	      f"{rnd(webhook_attempts_peak)} attempts/s with retries")
142	
143	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
144	# 6. Processor connection / outbound dependency sizing
145	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
146	# Each authorize makes 1 outbound call to an external processor. Processor
147	# round trip p50 ~250ms, p99 ~800ms. We size the outbound HTTP connection pool
148	# from Little's Law: concurrency = QPS x latency.
149	PROC_LATENCY_S = 0.30  # average end-to-end processor call
150	proc_concurrency_avg = avg_payment_qps * PROC_LATENCY_S
151	proc_concurrency_peak = peak_payment_qps * PROC_LATENCY_S
152	print("\n[6] OUTBOUND PROCESSOR CONCURRENCY (Little's Law)")
153	print(f"  Little's Law: concurrency = QPS x latency")
154	print(f"  avg = {avg_payment_qps:,.0f}/s x {PROC_LATENCY_S}s = "
155	      f"{proc_concurrency_avg:,.0f} in-flight calls")
156	print(f"  PEAK = {peak_payment_qps:,.0f}/s x {PROC_LATENCY_S}s = "
157	      f"{proc_concurrency_peak:,.0f} in-flight calls")
158	print(f"  ROUNDED: ~{proc_concurrency_peak/1000:.0f}K concurrent outbound "
159	      f"processor calls at peak")
160	
161	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
162	# 7. THE ONE DECISION-FORCING NUMBER + flip threshold
163	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
164	# The binding bottleneck is the LEDGER WRITE path: it's a strongly-consistent,
165	# double-entry ACID write that cannot be eventually consistent. A single
166	# Postgres primary tops out ~10-15K write TPS before fsync/WAL saturates.
167	PG_PRIMARY_TPS_CEILING = 12_000
168	print("\n[7] DECISION-FORCING NUMBER")
169	print(f"  Ledger row writes at PEAK: {ledger_rows_peak:,.0f}/s")
170	print(f"  Single Postgres primary write ceiling: ~{PG_PRIMARY_TPS_CEILING:,}/s")
171	flip = ledger_rows_peak > PG_PRIMARY_TPS_CEILING
172	print(f"  {ledger_rows_peak:,.0f} > {PG_PRIMARY_TPS_CEILING:,}? {flip}")
173	print(f"  -> {'SHARD the ledger by merchant/account_id' if flip else 'single primary OK'}")
174	shards_needed = -(-int(ledger_rows_peak) // PG_PRIMARY_TPS_CEILING)  # ceil
175	print(f"  Shards needed at peak = ceil({ledger_rows_peak:,.0f} / "
176	      f"{PG_PRIMARY_TPS_CEILING:,}) = {shards_needed}")
177	print(f"  FLIP THRESHOLD: single primary holds until ~{PG_PRIMARY_TPS_CEILING:,} "
178	      f"ledger rows/s; above that we shard.")
179	
180	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
181	# 8. Cost — unit economics ($/1M payments) + top drivers
182	# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
183	print("\n[8] COST — top drivers + unit economics")
184	# (a) Postgres ledger fleet: shards x (primary + 2 replicas), large instances.
185	PG_INSTANCE_MO = 3_000  # large managed Postgres instance / month
186	pg_instances = shards_needed * REPLICA
187	pg_cost = pg_instances * PG_INSTANCE_MO + ledger_storage_cost
188	print(f"  (a) Postgres ledger fleet: {shards_needed} shards x {REPLICA} nodes "
189	      f"= {pg_instances} instances x ${PG_INSTANCE_MO} + storage "
190	      f"= ${pg_cost:,.0f}/mo")
191	# (b) Webhook + worker compute fleet (Kafka + delivery workers).
192	WORKER_FLEET_MO = 40_000  # Kafka + worker autoscaling group, rough
193	print(f"  (b) Async fleet (Kafka + webhook workers): ~${WORKER_FLEET_MO:,}/mo")
194	# (c) Redis idempotency + cache cluster.
195	REDIS_MO = 15_000
196	print(f"  (c) Redis idempotency/cache cluster: ~${REDIS_MO:,}/mo")
197	total_infra = pg_cost + WORKER_FLEET_MO + REDIS_MO
198	# Note: external processor interchange fees are pass-through, NOT our infra cost.
199	print(f"  ---- total infra ~${total_infra:,.0f}/mo "
200	      f"(excludes pass-through interchange fees)")
201	cost_per_1m = total_infra / (txns_per_month / 1_000_000)
202	print(f"  UNIT ECONOMICS: ${total_infra:,.0f}/mo / "
203	      f"{txns_per_month/1e6:.0f}M payments = ${cost_per_1m:.2f} per 1M payments")
204	print(f"  ROUNDED: ~${total_infra/1000:.0f}K/mo infra, "
205	      f"~${cost_per_1m*1:.0f} per 1M payments (~${cost_per_1m/1000:.4f} each)")
206	
207	print("\n" + "=" * 64)
208	print("SUMMARY OF ROUNDED, TALKABLE NUMBERS")
209	print("=" * 64)
210	print(f"  Avg payment write QPS .......... {rnd(avg_payment_qps)}/s")
211	print(f"  PEAK payment write QPS ......... {rnd(peak_payment_qps)}/s (10x burst)")
212	print(f"  PEAK ledger-row writes ......... {rnd(ledger_rows_peak)}/s")
213	print(f"  Idempotency live keys (24h) .... {rnd(idemp_keys_stored)} (~{idemp_bytes/1e9:.0f}GB)")
214	print(f"  Ledger footprint ............... ~{ledger_footprint_month/1e12:.0f} TB/month")
215	print(f"  Ledger HOT (13mo) .............. ~{ledger_hot_tb:.0f} TB (~${ledger_storage_cost/1000:.0f}K/mo)")
216	print(f"  PEAK webhooks .................. {rnd(webhook_qps_peak)}/s ({rnd(webhook_attempts_peak)}/s w/ retries)")
217	print(f"  PEAK outbound processor calls .. ~{proc_concurrency_peak/1000:.0f}K concurrent")
218	print(f"  Ledger shards needed ........... {shards_needed} (flip at ~{PG_PRIMARY_TPS_CEILING/1000:.0f}K rows/s/primary)")
219	print(f"  Infra cost ..................... ~${total_infra/1000:.0f}K/mo")
220	print(f"  Unit cost ...................... ~${cost_per_1m:.0f} per 1M payments")

file.content:
"""Capacity model for a Payment Gateway System.

We model a mid-to-large payment gateway (Stripe-class but smaller): merchants
send us charges, we tokenize cards, call external processors (Visa/MC rails via
acquirers), persist a double-entry ledger, and fire async webhooks to merchants.

Everything here is the source of the rounded numbers used in the markdown.
"""

SEC_PER_DAY = 86_400
SEC_PER_MONTH = SEC_PER_DAY * 30


def rnd(n):
    """Round to a talkable magnitude."""
    if n >= 1_000_000_000:
        return f"~{n/1_000_000_000:.1f}B"
    if n >= 1_000_000:
        return f"~{n/1_000_000:.1f}M"
    if n >= 1_000:
        return f"~{n/1_000:.1f}K"
    return f"~{n:.1f}"


print("=" * 64)
print("PAYMENT GATEWAY — CAPACITY MODEL")
print("=" * 64)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 1. Transaction volume (the workload driver)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Assume: 2B payment transactions / month at steady state (large gateway,
# Stripe-class). A "transaction" = one authorize (+ usually a capture). Refunds
# + disputes are a small tail. This is the headline workload.
txns_per_month = 2_000_000_000
avg_payment_qps = txns_per_month / SEC_PER_MONTH
print("\n[1] PAYMENT WRITE QPS (the money path)")
print(f"  {txns_per_month:,} txns/month / {SEC_PER_MONTH:,}s")
print(f"  = {avg_payment_qps:,.0f} authorize/s average")

# Peak: payments are spiky (Black Friday, midnight subscription renewals,
# flash sales). Use a burst factor of 10x over the monthly average — payments
# are far burstier than reads.
BURST = 10
peak_payment_qps = avg_payment_qps * BURST
print(f"  burst factor {BURST}x -> {peak_payment_qps:,.0f} authorize/s PEAK")
print(f"  ROUNDED: avg {rnd(avg_payment_qps)}/s, PEAK {rnd(peak_payment_qps)}/s")

# Each authorize is typically followed by exactly one capture (immediate or
# delayed). Plus refunds ~3% of payments, disputes ~0.3%.
captures_qps = avg_payment_qps  # 1:1 with auth in the common case
refund_qps = avg_payment_qps * 0.03
dispute_qps = avg_payment_qps * 0.003
# Total ledger-mutating ops at peak:
ledger_writes_avg = avg_payment_qps + captures_qps + refund_qps + dispute_qps
ledger_writes_peak = ledger_writes_avg * BURST
print("\n[2] LEDGER WRITE QPS (double-entry — every money event)")
print(f"  auth {avg_payment_qps:,.0f} + capture {captures_qps:,.0f}"
      f" + refund {refund_qps:,.0f} + dispute {dispute_qps:,.1f}")
print(f"  = {ledger_writes_avg:,.0f} money-events/s avg")
print(f"  PEAK {ledger_writes_peak:,.0f}/s")
# Double-entry: each money event writes >= 2 ledger rows (debit + credit).
ledger_rows_peak = ledger_writes_peak * 2
print(f"  x2 rows (debit+credit) = {ledger_rows_peak:,.0f} ledger-row writes/s PEAK")
print(f"  ROUNDED: {rnd(ledger_writes_peak)} money-events/s, "
      f"{rnd(ledger_rows_peak)} ledger rows/s at peak")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 3. Idempotency dedup store sizing (sized from WRITE qps, TTL'd)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Every mutating request carries an idempotency key. We keep keys 24h.
IDEMP_TTL_H = 24
# All mutating requests (auth + capture + refund + dispute) at AVG (store is
# sized for sustained load, not instantaneous burst, but we keep headroom).
idemp_writes_avg = ledger_writes_avg
idemp_keys_stored = idemp_writes_avg * 3600 * IDEMP_TTL_H
# Each idempotency record: key (36B) + status + response snapshot (~1KB).
IDEMP_REC_BYTES = 1_200
idemp_bytes = idemp_keys_stored * IDEMP_REC_BYTES
print("\n[3] IDEMPOTENCY DEDUP STORE (Redis, 24h TTL)")
print(f"  {idemp_writes_avg:,.0f} keys/s x {3600*IDEMP_TTL_H:,}s")
print(f"  = {idemp_keys_stored:,.0f} live keys")
print(f"  x {IDEMP_REC_BYTES}B/rec = {idemp_bytes/1e9:.1f} GB")
print(f"  ROUNDED: {rnd(idemp_keys_stored)} live keys, ~{idemp_bytes/1e9:.0f} GB in Redis")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 4. Ledger storage (the LARGEST, fastest-growing table) — full footprint
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Each ledger entry row: ~300 bytes raw (ids, amount, currency, account refs,
# timestamps, txn ref). Two rows per money event.
LEDGER_ROW_BYTES = 300
ledger_rows_per_month = ledger_writes_avg * 2 * SEC_PER_MONTH
raw_ledger_bytes_month = ledger_rows_per_month * LEDGER_ROW_BYTES
# Real footprint multiplier: indexes (PK + account_id + txn_id + time) ~2x,
# replication factor 3, WAL + compaction headroom ~1.5x.
INDEX_MULT = 2.0
REPLICA = 3
WAL_MULT = 1.5
footprint_mult = INDEX_MULT * REPLICA * WAL_MULT
ledger_footprint_month = raw_ledger_bytes_month * footprint_mult
print("\n[4] LEDGER STORAGE (largest table — full footprint)")
print(f"  {ledger_rows_per_month:,.0f} rows/month x {LEDGER_ROW_BYTES}B")
print(f"  = {raw_ledger_bytes_month/1e12:.2f} TB raw/month")
print(f"  x footprint mult {footprint_mult:.1f} "
      f"(idx {INDEX_MULT} x replica {REPLICA} x wal {WAL_MULT})")
print(f"  = {ledger_footprint_month/1e12:.2f} TB/month full footprint")
# Retention: regulatory (card scheme + tax) typically 7 years for the ledger,
# but HOT (queryable in Postgres) we keep ~13 months; older partitions go to S3.
HOT_MONTHS = 13
ledger_hot_tb = ledger_footprint_month / 1e12 * HOT_MONTHS
print(f"  HOT {HOT_MONTHS} months in Postgres = {ledger_hot_tb:.1f} TB")
# Monthly $ for hot ledger storage on cloud block storage (~$0.10/GB-mo SSD).
SSD_GB_MO = 0.10
ledger_storage_cost = ledger_hot_tb * 1000 * SSD_GB_MO
print(f"  hot storage $ = {ledger_hot_tb:.1f}TB x 1000 x ${SSD_GB_MO}/GB-mo "
      f"= ${ledger_storage_cost:,.0f}/mo")
print(f"  ROUNDED: ~{ledger_footprint_month/1e12:.0f} TB/month footprint, "
      f"~{ledger_hot_tb:.0f} TB hot, ~${ledger_storage_cost/1000:.0f}K/mo SSD")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 5. Webhook fan-out (async, at-least-once)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Each payment lifecycle emits multiple webhook events: auth, capture, and
# possibly refund/dispute. Assume ~3 webhook deliveries per payment on average.
WEBHOOKS_PER_PAYMENT = 3
webhook_qps_avg = avg_payment_qps * WEBHOOKS_PER_PAYMENT
webhook_qps_peak = webhook_qps_avg * BURST
print("\n[5] WEBHOOK DELIVERY QPS (async, at-least-once)")
print(f"  {avg_payment_qps:,.0f} payments/s x {WEBHOOKS_PER_PAYMENT} events")
print(f"  = {webhook_qps_avg:,.0f} webhooks/s avg, PEAK {webhook_qps_peak:,.0f}/s")
# Retry amplification: ~5% of endpoints are slow/down and get retried up to
# 8 times with backoff -> amplification factor.
RETRY_FRACTION = 0.05
RETRY_ATTEMPTS = 8
retry_amp = 1 + RETRY_FRACTION * (RETRY_ATTEMPTS - 1)
webhook_attempts_peak = webhook_qps_peak * retry_amp
print(f"  retry amp {retry_amp:.2f}x ({RETRY_FRACTION:.0%} endpoints x "
      f"{RETRY_ATTEMPTS} attempts)")
print(f"  = {webhook_attempts_peak:,.0f} delivery attempts/s PEAK")
print(f"  ROUNDED: {rnd(webhook_qps_peak)} webhooks/s peak, "
      f"{rnd(webhook_attempts_peak)} attempts/s with retries")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 6. Processor connection / outbound dependency sizing
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Each authorize makes 1 outbound call to an external processor. Processor
# round trip p50 ~250ms, p99 ~800ms. We size the outbound HTTP connection pool
# from Little's Law: concurrency = QPS x latency.
PROC_LATENCY_S = 0.30  # average end-to-end processor call
proc_concurrency_avg = avg_payment_qps * PROC_LATENCY_S
proc_concurrency_peak = peak_payment_qps * PROC_LATENCY_S
print("\n[6] OUTBOUND PROCESSOR CONCURRENCY (Little's Law)")
print(f"  Little's Law: concurrency = QPS x latency")
print(f"  avg = {avg_payment_qps:,.0f}/s x {PROC_LATENCY_S}s = "
      f"{proc_concurrency_avg:,.0f} in-flight calls")
print(f"  PEAK = {peak_payment_qps:,.0f}/s x {PROC_LATENCY_S}s = "
      f"{proc_concurrency_peak:,.0f} in-flight calls")
print(f"  ROUNDED: ~{proc_concurrency_peak/1000:.0f}K concurrent outbound "
      f"processor calls at peak")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 7. THE ONE DECISION-FORCING NUMBER + flip threshold
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# The binding bottleneck is the LEDGER WRITE path: it's a strongly-consistent,
# double-entry ACID write that cannot be eventually consistent. A single
# Postgres primary tops out ~10-15K write TPS before fsync/WAL saturates.
PG_PRIMARY_TPS_CEILING = 12_000
print("\n[7] DECISION-FORCING NUMBER")
print(f"  Ledger row writes at PEAK: {ledger_rows_peak:,.0f}/s")
print(f"  Single Postgres primary write ceiling: ~{PG_PRIMARY_TPS_CEILING:,}/s")
flip = ledger_rows_peak > PG_PRIMARY_TPS_CEILING
print(f"  {ledger_rows_peak:,.0f} > {PG_PRIMARY_TPS_CEILING:,}? {flip}")
print(f"  -> {'SHARD the ledger by merchant/account_id' if flip else 'single primary OK'}")
shards_needed = -(-int(ledger_rows_peak) // PG_PRIMARY_TPS_CEILING)  # ceil
print(f"  Shards needed at peak = ceil({ledger_rows_peak:,.0f} / "
      f"{PG_PRIMARY_TPS_CEILING:,}) = {shards_needed}")
print(f"  FLIP THRESHOLD: single primary holds until ~{PG_PRIMARY_TPS_CEILING:,} "
      f"ledger rows/s; above that we shard.")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 8. Cost — unit economics ($/1M payments) + top drivers
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
print("\n[8] COST — top drivers + unit economics")
# (a) Postgres ledger fleet: shards x (primary + 2 replicas), large instances.
PG_INSTANCE_MO = 3_000  # large managed Postgres instance / month
pg_instances = shards_needed * REPLICA
pg_cost = pg_instances * PG_INSTANCE_MO + ledger_storage_cost
print(f"  (a) Postgres ledger fleet: {shards_needed} shards x {REPLICA} nodes "
      f"= {pg_instances} instances x ${PG_INSTANCE_MO} + storage "
      f"= ${pg_cost:,.0f}/mo")
# (b) Webhook + worker compute fleet (Kafka + delivery workers).
WORKER_FLEET_MO = 40_000  # Kafka + worker autoscaling group, rough
print(f"  (b) Async fleet (Kafka + webhook workers): ~${WORKER_FLEET_MO:,}/mo")
# (c) Redis idempotency + cache cluster.
REDIS_MO = 15_000
print(f"  (c) Redis idempotency/cache cluster: ~${REDIS_MO:,}/mo")
total_infra = pg_cost + WORKER_FLEET_MO + REDIS_MO
# Note: external processor interchange fees are pass-through, NOT our infra cost.
print(f"  ---- total infra ~${total_infra:,.0f}/mo "
      f"(excludes pass-through interchange fees)")
cost_per_1m = total_infra / (txns_per_month / 1_000_000)
print(f"  UNIT ECONOMICS: ${total_infra:,.0f}/mo / "
      f"{txns_per_month/1e6:.0f}M payments = ${cost_per_1m:.2f} per 1M payments")
print(f"  ROUNDED: ~${total_infra/1000:.0f}K/mo infra, "
      f"~${cost_per_1m*1:.0f} per 1M payments (~${cost_per_1m/1000:.4f} each)")

print("\n" + "=" * 64)
print("SUMMARY OF ROUNDED, TALKABLE NUMBERS")
print("=" * 64)
print(f"  Avg payment write QPS .......... {rnd(avg_payment_qps)}/s")
print(f"  PEAK payment write QPS ......... {rnd(peak_payment_qps)}/s (10x burst)")
print(f"  PEAK ledger-row writes ......... {rnd(ledger_rows_peak)}/s")
print(f"  Idempotency live keys (24h) .... {rnd(idemp_keys_stored)} (~{idemp_bytes/1e9:.0f}GB)")
print(f"  Ledger footprint ............... ~{ledger_footprint_month/1e12:.0f} TB/month")
print(f"  Ledger HOT (13mo) .............. ~{ledger_hot_tb:.0f} TB (~${ledger_storage_cost/1000:.0f}K/mo)")
print(f"  PEAK webhooks .................. {rnd(webhook_qps_peak)}/s ({rnd(webhook_attempts_peak)}/s w/ retries)")
print(f"  PEAK outbound processor calls .. ~{proc_concurrency_peak/1000:.0f}K concurrent")
print(f"  Ledger shards needed ........... {shards_needed} (flip at ~{PG_PRIMARY_TPS_CEILING/1000:.0f}K rows/s/primary)")
print(f"  Infra cost ..................... ~${total_infra/1000:.0f}K/mo")
print(f"  Unit cost ...................... ~${cost_per_1m:.0f} per 1M payments")

================================================================
PAYMENT GATEWAY — CAPACITY MODEL
================================================================

[1] PAYMENT WRITE QPS (the money path)
  2,000,000,000 txns/month / 2,592,000s
  = 772 authorize/s average
  burst factor 10x -> 7,716 authorize/s PEAK
  ROUNDED: avg ~771.6/s, PEAK ~7.7K/s

[2] LEDGER WRITE QPS (double-entry — every money event)
  auth 772 + capture 772 + refund 23 + dispute 2.3
  = 1,569 money-events/s avg
  PEAK 15,687/s
  x2 rows (debit+credit) = 31,373 ledger-row writes/s PEAK
  ROUNDED: ~15.7K money-events/s, ~31.4K ledger rows/s at peak

[3] IDEMPOTENCY DEDUP STORE (Redis, 24h TTL)
  1,569 keys/s x 86,400s
  = 135,533,333 live keys
  x 1200B/rec = 162.6 GB
  ROUNDED: ~135.5M live keys, ~163 GB in Redis

[4] LEDGER STORAGE (largest table — full footprint)
  8,132,000,000 rows/month x 300B
  = 2.44 TB raw/month
  x footprint mult 9.0 (idx 2.0 x replica 3 x wal 1.5)
  = 21.96 TB/month full footprint
  HOT 13 months in Postgres = 285.4 TB
  hot storage $ = 285.4TB x 1000 x $0.1/GB-mo = $28,543/mo
  ROUNDED: ~22 TB/month footprint, ~285 TB hot, ~$29K/mo SSD

[5] WEBHOOK DELIVERY QPS (async, at-least-once)
  772 payments/s x 3 events
  = 2,315 webhooks/s avg, PEAK 23,148/s
  retry amp 1.35x (5% endpoints x 8 attempts)
  = 31,250 delivery attempts/s PEAK
  ROUNDED: ~23.1K webhooks/s peak, ~31.2K attempts/s with retries

[6] OUTBOUND PROCESSOR CONCURRENCY (Little's Law)
  Little's Law: concurrency = QPS x latency
  avg = 772/s x 0.3s = 231 in-flight calls
  PEAK = 7,716/s x 0.3s = 2,315 in-flight calls
  ROUNDED: ~2K concurrent outbound processor calls at peak

[7] DECISION-FORCING NUMBER
  Ledger row writes at PEAK: 31,373/s
  Single Postgres primary write ceiling: ~12,000/s
  31,373 > 12,000? True
  -> SHARD the ledger by merchant/account_id
  Shards needed at peak = ceil(31,373 / 12,000) = 3
  FLIP THRESHOLD: single primary holds until ~12,000 ledger rows/s; above that we shard.

[8] COST — top drivers + unit economics
  (a) Postgres ledger fleet: 3 shards x 3 nodes = 9 instances x $3000 + storage = $55,543/mo
  (b) Async fleet (Kafka + webhook workers): ~$40,000/mo
  (c) Redis idempotency/cache cluster: ~$15,000/mo
  ---- total infra ~$110,543/mo (excludes pass-through interchange fees)
  UNIT ECONOMICS: $110,543/mo / 2000M payments = $55.27 per 1M payments
  ROUNDED: ~$111K/mo infra, ~$55 per 1M payments (~$0.0553 each)

================================================================
SUMMARY OF ROUNDED, TALKABLE NUMBERS
================================================================
  Avg payment write QPS .......... ~771.6/s
  PEAK payment write QPS ......... ~7.7K/s (10x burst)
  PEAK ledger-row writes ......... ~31.4K/s
  Idempotency live keys (24h) .... ~135.5M (~163GB)
  Ledger footprint ............... ~22 TB/month
  Ledger HOT (13mo) .............. ~285 TB (~$29K/mo)
  PEAK webhooks .................. ~23.1K/s (~31.2K/s w/ retries)
  PEAK outbound processor calls .. ~2K concurrent
  Ledger shards needed ........... 3 (flip at ~12K rows/s/primary)
  Infra cost ..................... ~$111K/mo
  Unit cost ...................... ~$55 per 1M payments

The existing capacity.py runs cleanly and produces a coherent, self-consistent set of numbers. I'll keep it (it's well-built and matches all the emphasis areas). Now I have all the numbers I need:

- Avg payment write QPS: ~770/s; PEAK ~7.7K/s (10x burst)
- Peak ledger-row writes: ~31.4K/s → shard into 3 (flip at ~12K rows/s/primary)
- Read:write — writes are the risk, not volume
- Idempotency: ~135.5M live keys, ~163GB Redis
- Ledger footprint: ~22 TB/month, ~285 TB hot (~$29K/mo)
- Peak webhooks: ~23.1K/s (~31.2K/s with retries)
- Peak outbound processor concurrency: ~2K
- Infra: ~$111K/mo, ~$55/1M payments
